Search APIs have traditionally used POST whenever filters become complex. While it works, it comes with trade-offs: poor cacheability, reduced observability, and less alignment with HTTP semantics.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image used under fair use. Sourced from Google. Not owned by the author.The emerging HTTP QUERY method is designed specifically for this problem. It allows complex search requests while preserving the semantics of a safe, read-only operation.
The Problem with POST-Based Search
A typical search endpoint looks like this:
POST /users/search
Content-Type: application/json{
"roles": ["ADMIN", "MANAGER"],
"status": "ACTIVE",
"createdAfter": "2025-01-01",
"page": 0,
"size": 20
}
Although the operation only reads data, it uses POST, which is intended for creating or processing resources.
This creates several challenges:

• Search operations look like write operations.

• HTTP caches generally do not cache POST responses.

• API gateways and proxies cannot easily optimize requests.

• Logs and monitoring tools lose visibility into search intent.

• Clients cannot safely retry requests based on HTTP semantics.
